title: Release Notes & Frequently Asked Questions | Respiratory Illnesses | CDC
text:
n wastewater over time, public health officials can determine if infections are increasing or decreasing in an area. More detailed methods can be found in About Wastewater Data | National Wastewater Surveillance System | CDC. 
Wastewater findings may diverge from health outcome metrics, like tracking of emergency department visits, for multiple reasons. For example, wastewater data can be used as an early indicator for monitoring the spread of infection before people have symptoms or require medical care. Wastewater levels may go up (or down) before we see changes in metrics like emergency department use. 
Also, wastewater testing cannot determine the source of the viruses detected. For influenza, wastewater detections do not necessarily indicate human cases (it could be from an infected animal or animal product, like milk from an infected cow), potentially leading to the wastewater findings differing from what we are seeing in our heath outcome metrics that pertain to humans. 
Finally, wastewater testing is done on human waste disposed of through the municipal sewage system. Wastewater data will not reflect populations whose waste is disposed of outside the wastewater system, such as people wearing diapers, or households on septic tanks or otherwise not connected to a municipal sewer system.
